about.body: UX design student at SCAD, product intern at Swipey. I design in Figma and ship working software.

aboutPage.heroBody: UX design student at SCAD, product intern at Swipey. I design in Figma and ship working software. Trained as an artist first, and it still shapes everything.
aboutPage.whoIAm: A third culture kid, currently surviving Savannah. I picked up a camera before I picked up Figma, and I still think in frames: who, what, when, where. At SCAD I study UX. Outside class I build various experiences such as — a macOS app, this site, and a system that runs my days.

experience.desc: Designing Swipey's admin mobile app from scratch: research, wireframes, and high-fidelity screens on their existing design system. I also rebuilt the core flows as an interactive demo, with onboarding questions that qualify incoming leads.
Designed sticker packs for the Vircle × Minions: Despicable Me 4 collaboration, printed in the packaging of every card sold. I also made the hero images and prototyped a school landing page.
Chaired the annual sixth-form art exhibition. I ran every department, handled the logistics, and produced all the branding and marketing.
On the marketing team making social content. I started and ran the company TikTok, from filming and editing to figuring out what actually got views.

professionalActivities.desc: Figma's three-day design challenge. I used it to build Accord.
48-hour weather app for pet owners, built with Claude — judged by Google.
Sharpening my Figma skills at Figma Fridays.
Redesigned the Passio Go app in Figma Make, poking at how design changes when AI is in the loop.

education.org: Savannah College of Art and Design (SCAD)
education.desc: BFA in UX Design, freshman year. Coursework across interaction design, user research, prototyping, and design systems.

caseStudy.defaultProblem: This project focused on understanding user needs and translating them into a cohesive design solution. Through research, ideation, and iteration, the final product addresses real problems with intentional design decisions.
caseStudy.defaultRole: Led end-to-end UX design including research planning, synthesis, interaction design, and high-fidelity prototyping.

sitePages.label: PLAY
sitePages.constructionBody: This section is being built around playable web games and interaction experiments. It will be live once the games are ready.

caseStudies.title: Franklin's
caseStudies.oneliner: The experience starts before you walk in.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: Franklin's is great once you're inside. The job was to close the gap between the website and what people actually walked into.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Team lead on a five-person UXDG 101 project. I ran the research, the fieldwork, the card sort, the IA, and the final Figma prototype.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Interviews with baristas and customers, plus a SCAD student survey. The site was outdated, the service model unclear, and none of the in-person warmth made it online.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Their warmth was real. It just wasn't reaching people before they arrived.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: People don't think in business categories, they think in tasks. A card sort surfaced four groups — Menu, Order, About, Contact — and that became the nav.
caseStudies.processHeadline: I rebuilt the homepage around the practical questions first: seating, service style, hours. Navigation dropped to four items, and a full test order ran start to finish without anyone hitting a wall.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: Task-based navigation, a rebuilt homepage, a cleaner menu, and an order flow you can actually follow.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: The gap was an information problem, not a design one. Next, I'd put digital touchpoints in the store itself.

caseStudies.title: Albers
caseStudies.oneliner: What if 'interaction of color' was taken literally?
caseStudies.problemHeadline: Colour theory gets taught with static swatches. Albers turns each perceptual effect into an instrument you can push until it clicks. It shipped twice: native on macOS in SwiftUI, and as the web build below.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole designer and developer. Concept, UI in Figma, built in SwiftUI with Claude Code.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: The brief said 'interaction of colours.' Most people went editorial; I kept catching on the word interaction. Albers's argument that colour has no fixed identity is a UI problem as much as an art one.
caseStudies.pullQuote: When the brief said 'interaction of colors,' I took the word interaction literally.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: The first submission was a lean prototype. Spring break was the space to add modes, tighten the interaction model, and build an identity: an amber terminal HUD, borrowed from instruments that measure rather than create.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Figma set the visual direction, then iteration happened inside the app with Claude Code handling implementation while I focused on how the interaction should feel.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: Nine modes, each isolating one perceptual colour phenomenon with real-time controls. Built in SwiftUI with AppKit for macOS 13+.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Claude Code forced me to be precise about intent upfront, and committing hard to the HUD made every other decision easier. I'd like to publish it on the Mac App Store as a real teaching tool.
caseStudies.solutionEmbedCalloutBody: This is the actual live app — the web port of the macOS build, not a mockup. Interact with it right here, or open it in its own tab.
caseStudies.solutionEmbedCalloutTitle: The shipped Albers build is embedded below.

caseStudies.title: Wander
caseStudies.problemHeadline: Google Maps optimises for fastest, Strava for performance. 28 of 34 people surveyed said they walk to clear their head, but none called it intentional. It happens in spite of the tools.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Designer on a team of two. Co-owned research, synthesis, concept, and UI in Figma across the full 48 hours.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: A 15-person survey on walking and mid-walk phone habits, backed by attention restoration theory (Kaplan, 1995) and Stanford's work on walking and creative cognition (Oppezzo & Schwartz, 2014). People already walk to decompress; the tools get in the way.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Walking already works. We just need to protect it from the phone.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: How do you build an app whose job is to get you off the app? Two moments to serve, time with no destination and a destination with no rush, both needing the same reset.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Visual direction from Cosmos, wireframes in Figma, AI to build the prototype fast, then back to Figma for the final creatives.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A prototype covering both modes, the prompt reveal, the active walk and the end screen. Once you start walking it drops to a lock screen widget: prompt and distance, no steps, no streaks.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Building something that works by getting out of the way runs against every instinct in product design. Next I'd sharpen the route logic behind both modes.
caseStudies.solutionEmbedCalloutBody: This is the actual live app, not a mockup. You can interact with it here, then open it in a separate tab if you want the full browser context.
caseStudies.solutionEmbedCalloutTitle: The shipped Wander build is embedded below.

caseStudies.title: LoomLearn
caseStudies.oneliner: One learning space for students who think differently.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: LoomLearn puts Cornell notes, Pomodoro timers, mind maps, spider charts and flashcards on one drag-and-drop canvas. The problem was never the tools, it was switching between them.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole designer across research, UX, UI, and prototyping.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Digital learners, especially those with dyslexia, struggle with context-switching rather than the tools themselves. I mapped two personas: Emma, a first-year recently diagnosed with dyslexia, and Aadi, preparing for IGCSEs.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Building for accessibility first made the product stronger for everyone.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: The drag-and-drop system came straight from Scratch: familiar, low-threshold, and customisable without adding complexity.
caseStudies.processHeadline: A minimal palette to reduce cognitive load, multiple workspaces to keep subjects separate, and every technique as a draggable icon rather than a menu item.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: One desktop canvas holding Cornell notes, mind maps, to-do lists, spider charts, flashcards and Pomodoro timers, rearrangeable per session.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Designing for accessibility first made the product stronger for everyone. I'd revisit onboarding so the widgets explain themselves.

caseStudies.title: HelpOH
caseStudies.oneliner: Connecting homes to trusted help, and workers to fair work.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: HelpOH connects people with vetted professionals for household services while giving workers legal protection, training and steady income. Built for Malaysia's growing economy.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole designer across research, UX strategy, UI, and prototyping.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Users struggle to find reliable, affordable help; workers face inconsistent pay and little legal protection. I mapped Farah, a busy professional, and Arif, a skilled tradesman, to serve both sides equitably.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Two very different users, one platform — the design had to serve both without compromising either.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: Booking flows built for transparency and speed, borrowing trust signals from Grab that the Southeast Asian market already reads.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Green and white to feel trustworthy and familiar, with the whole booking flow — service, location, date, time — kept on a single surface.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A desktop platform with fast search-and-book, transparent pricing and clear service categories. Clarity over density.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Designing for two very different users at once was the real exercise. Next I'd build the worker dashboard properly, with job management and training.

caseStudies.title: Atom OS
caseStudies.oneliner: A phone OS that strips your screen down to what actually matters.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: Atom OS strips the smartphone back to four intentional widgets. The goal was to make the phone feel deliberately limited, not broken.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole designer across concept, UI, and prototyping.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: The idea came out of exam season, where existing solutions either deleted apps outright or were too rigid to adjust. I mapped two users: students under academic pressure and professionals with social media anxiety.
caseStudies.pullQuote: The hardest part wasn't adding features — it was deciding what to withhold.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: What does a dumb phone mode look like without giving up utility? Black and white to psychologically reduce the pull of the screen, and limitation that reads as designed.
caseStudies.processHeadline: The gradient in the screen time dashboard signals which apps help and which hurt without being prescriptive, and Focus mode stays user-configured to preserve autonomy.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A four-widget home screen with screen time visualisation, usage breakdowns, and a toggleable Focus mode that blocks chosen apps for set periods.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: The hard part wasn't adding features, it was deciding what to withhold. I'd revisit onboarding so people understand the philosophy before they start.

caseStudies.title: South China Sea
caseStudies.oneliner: What ordinary people don't know is already shaping their lives.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: A large-scale mixed media installation on the South China Sea conflict, seen through public naivety. Built as a detective board of cyanotypes, photograms, coffee-aged maps and original photography.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole artist: concept, research, photography, darkroom work, and installation.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: I started from Gaza and Ukraine, then narrowed to the South China Sea. Close to home, under-discussed, and layered with economic, territorial and military tension.
caseStudies.pullQuote: What ordinary people don't know is already shaping their lives.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: The detective board became the structural metaphor, red threads mirroring how the public tries and fails to connect the dots. Four photo series covered China's history, espionage, surveillance, and the conflict's root causes.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Cyanotypes brought in the blue of the sea, photograms stripped colour and context so figures stay unidentifiable, and coffee aging gave the archival maps material authenticity.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A 7ft × 3ft detective board of original photography, cyanotypes, photograms, coffee-aged map prints and handwritten notes, all connected by red string.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: The board let viewers discover the connections instead of being told them. The piece lived in the visual; there's a version that also hits the body, through sound or something ambient.

caseStudies.title: Faces of Power
caseStudies.oneliner: What does power look like up close?
caseStudies.problemHeadline: A mixed media project on how leaders make decisions that affect millions. Close-up portraiture, interviews and layered physical materials, pushing at privacy, intimacy and authority.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole artist: concept, photography, material fabrication, and installation.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: An extension of the South China Sea piece, moving from geopolitics to the people behind it. I wanted to know what it means to hold power, and what hides behind a public face.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Removing colour and context made the images feel universal — anyone could be in power.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: Close-up portraits and interviews asking the discomforting personal questions powerful people are rarely asked publicly, with masks as a recurring motif after Brian Cattle.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Multiple identities layered into single images using acetate and a custom-built A4 lightbox. Gelli printing added a propaganda aesthetic; photograms stripped context entirely.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A multi-part installation of Mod Roc masks, acetate lightbox prints, gelli prints and photograms, each on a different facet of how power is constructed, projected and concealed.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Removing colour and context made the images feel universal: anyone could be in power. I'd push the interviews further and make them part of the installation itself.

caseStudies.title: Oracle
caseStudies.problemHeadline: My first fully self-directed project. No brief, no client, no constraints beyond my own taste, treating branding as world-building rather than identity design.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole creative director across concept, product design, and marketing.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: I mapped the Matrix's visual language, glitch aesthetics, green code and retro-digital imagery, onto current digital culture: nostalgia, memes, lo-fi irony. The audience wasn't a demographic, it was an attitude.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Art allows open interpretation. Branding has to close down meaning enough to be actionable.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: 'For trendsetters by trend haters.' The slogan had to make the brand culturally aware while rejecting cultural positioning, sitting just outside the usual frame of reference.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Marketing leaned retro-digital, 'Buy Oracle' on old TVs and vintage monitors, using trending meme formats and nostalgia to build presence with no paid placement.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A complete brand world: six t-shirt designs, logo and identity, and a campaign of social graphics, vintage screen mockups and a Coming Soon teaser, all produced in Figma.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Oracle taught me the difference between art and branding. Meaningful branding needs purpose, not just aesthetics. Extended, it would need lore people can be part of, not just wear.

caseStudies.title: SOHO
caseStudies.oneliner: A sixth form art exhibition as diverse as the districts it's named after.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: SOHO was the 2024 Sixth Form Art Exhibition, planned and run by students. Named after the SOHO districts of London and New York, it pulled art, fashion and music under one identity.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Chair of the exhibition: organising, delegating, managing tasks, and leading all branding.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: I treated SOHO as a cultural zone, many creative voices under one name. That became the anchor: every artist is different, but the exhibition holds them together.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Each artist is different. The exhibition holds them together.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: The problem was representing sensory diversity visually. Fragmented sensory imagery, eyes, mouths and ears, combined with gradient blobs to carry the plurality.
caseStudies.processHeadline: A bold typographic lockup against sensory collage kept the branding layered and alive rather than institutional, with the poster, email banner and postcards designed as one system.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A full exhibition identity: A3 poster, email banner, and a postcard series featuring each participating artist's work, sold for charity.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Chairing it taught me design leadership is as much coordination as craft. Getting peers to hit deadlines was harder than the visual work. Next time I'd set a production timeline from day one.

caseStudies.title: The Lines in Between
caseStudies.oneliner: A tapestry of cultures, stitched across the transit lines of Kuala Lumpur.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: Given the theme "Union", I turned to Kuala Lumpur, where Chinatown, Kampung Baru and Brickfields sit minutes apart on the MRT and generations apart in culture. The question wasn't whether they're united, but whether proximity is the same thing as union.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: A-Level Photography final piece at Garden International School, KL. Solo: research, photography, textile experimentation, and construction.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Michael Wolf's Tokyo Compression reframed the project: strangers physically touching, completely disconnected. Cartier-Bresson and Tony Ray-Jones shaped how I photographed people; Shonibare, Zangewa and the Quilts of Gee's Bend shaped the textiles.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: Street photography could show difference, but not the tension of three communities coexisting for a century without merging. The MRT map became the second surface: the one system connecting every pocket of the city while belonging to none of them.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Every day, thousands of strangers share the same carriage and depart in different directions.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Shooting across Chinatown, KL Sentral, Kampung Baru and Brickfields taught me how close to get. A long lens got spotted, so I shot short and from the hip. Screen printing failed on the map's fine lines, so the network was digitally printed on calico, then stitched: French knots for every station, chain stitch for the rail lines in their real colours.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: An A0 calico tapestry. The KL MRT network as the backbone, cultural patterns from all three communities filling the space between the lines, and the strongest photographs mounted in embroidery hoops where each enclave sits. Everything connected, nothing quite touching.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Awarded an A*. The rough print quality on calico, which I first read as a flaw, started to feel honest: not every union is clean. I'd push closer to the individuals inside each community, not just their environments.

caseStudies.title: bowl
caseStudies.oneliner: A Malaysian inspired reddition of Fruit Ninja
caseStudies.solution: This game explores gesture-based interaction by replacing mouse input with webcam hand tracking. The focus was on making the mechanic feel immediate, playful, and easy to understand.
